lens.included_activities: Independent LMM manufacturers and rebuilders of locomotives, freight and passenger cars, rapid-transit equipment, railcar parts and equipment, and track-maintenance machinery sold repeatedly to external railroads, transit agencies, lessors, fleets, or OEMs.
lens.excluded_activities: Rail operations, repair-only shops, lessors without manufacturing, captive plants, shells, and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat new-build, rebuild, component, replacement, and fleet-program revenue under bids and long-term contracts, supported by engineering, safety compliance, inspection, documentation, warranty, and parts service.

a.bridge: BLS directly measures the broader four-digit rolling-stock industry and reports welders at 11.26% of employment, while other production roles are also material. I map bid and specification review, engineering search, configuration documentation, planning, procurement, quality-data analysis, warranty triage, and parts support as exposed; welding, forming, machining, assembly, painting, inspection execution, testing, and material handling are less substitutable. The four-digit bridge is close in industry scope but still lacks wage-weighted task and current-automation detail.
a.rationale: High labor intensity creates a meaningful gross pool, though heavy physical fabrication and assembly restrict the exposed share.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted task inventories from 8-12 LMM rolling-stock and component makers, including current CAD/CAM, ERP, QMS, and shop automation, would materially revise the estimate.
caveats: Complete vehicles, parts, and rebuilders have different labor mixes.

rho.rationale: Specification extraction, bid support, document search, planning, parts identification, quality trending, and warranty triage are implementable within five years. FRA rules, customer acceptance, public procurement, legacy systems, low-volume configurations, product liability, and physical validation slow realization.
rho.change_evidence: Measured pilots across tender response, engineering change, quality records, parts support, scheduling, and warranty at representative firms would move the estimate.
caveats: Bounded judgment rather than observed adoption.

e.rationale: Independent parts, rebuild, maintenance-equipment, and niche vehicle makers can have repeat external programs. Large strategic plants, captive facilities, repair-only shops, leasing entities, project-only fabricators, extreme customer concentration, and Buy America limitations reduce eligibility.
e.change_evidence: Classify the injected 52 candidates by ownership, manufacturing versus repair or leasing, repeat revenue, contract backlog, customer concentration, certifications, and EBITDA.
caveats: Injected count uses an auto-parts margin bridge and is estimated.
Establishments may not equal transferable firms.

s5.bridge: Federal Reserve owner-age data cover small businesses generally, not rolling-stock control transfers. I use age as succession pressure and discount for internal succession, closures, strategic subsidiaries, long-cycle public contracts, customer approvals, product liability, and capital intensity. Consolidation by component groups may increase strategic sales.
s5.rationale: Succession and fragmented component niches support transfers, but vehicle makers are concentrated and customer, certification, and backlog diligence makes qualifying deals uncommon.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook/CapIQ ownership changes, FTA supplier and award data, Surface Transportation Board records where relevant, and rail-sector M&A adviser interviews since 2015 would establish a denominator-based rate.

q.rationale: Safety qualification, installed-base compatibility, long product lives, specialized tooling, and scarce suppliers support retention. Public bids, railroad and lessor procurement, open-book contracts, escalation clauses, and OEM cost-downs pass some benefits through.
q.change_evidence: Fleet and public-agency contracts, bid histories, price-adjustment and cost-down clauses, parts margins, warranty economics, and customer interviews would directly test retention.
caveats: No public contract sample measures automation pass-through.
Aftermarket components may retain more than publicly bid new vehicles.

d5.bridge: FTA's FY2026 Rail Vehicle Replacement Program offers $166.05 million and explicitly considers cars reaching the end of useful life within five years, which measures one public funding channel rather than total rolling-stock quantity. I translate documented replacement need plus freight-car, locomotive, transit, and track-equipment cycles into modest five-year real growth. The proxy may understate larger state and local procurement or overstate realized orders if matching funds fail.
d5.rationale: Aging fleets, funded transit replacement, safety requirements, freight and passenger modernization, and track-maintenance needs support demand, while freight cycles, public-budget timing, and project delays create downside.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year agency capital plans, freight-car orders and retirements, locomotive rebuild plans, lessor capex, FTA award drawdowns, and manufacturer backlogs would materially revise the range.
caveats: Federal grant funding is only part of demand and not delivered units.
Passenger, freight, locomotive, and maintenance equipment cycles differ.

o.rationale: Retained demand requires accountable manufacturers to fabricate, assemble, rebuild, inspect, test, and certify physical rolling stock. Digital twins and autonomous operations change features and workflows but cannot supply vehicles; imports and railroad in-house rebuilds can shift operator share.
o.change_evidence: Railroad and transit make-buy studies, import and Buy America trends, in-house rebuild capacity, and standardized modular vehicle sourcing would move the estimate.
caveats: Operator share may shift to foreign or captive plants.
This is separate from labor automation within manufacturers.

narrative.executive_view: Rolling stock combines high labor intensity, durable physical replacement demand, and strong safety and compatibility barriers. AI can improve bidding, engineering, planning, and quality administration, while welding and assembly cap substitution.
narrative.ai_impact: Uses include tender and specification extraction, engineering and parts search, configuration records, work instructions, scheduling, procurement, quality trend analysis, warranty triage, and fleet support. Forming, welding, machining, assembly, painting, inspection, and dynamic testing remain physical.
narrative.value_capture: Safety qualification, installed-base compatibility, long life cycles, tooling, and scarce suppliers protect gains. Public procurement, railroad and lessor bargaining, open-book terms, and escalation clauses share savings.
narrative.firm_availability: The estimated population includes attractive parts, rebuild, and maintenance-equipment niches, but large strategic plants, captive shops, leasing, repair-only models, customer concentration, and project economics reduce eligibility.
narrative.demand_durability: Aging passenger and freight fleets, replacement grants, safety standards, modernization, and track maintenance sustain physical demand. Public-budget delays, freight cycles, imports, and long order timing create volatility.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Public procurement, Buy America compliance, product liability, welding and quality capacity, customer concentration, cyclical freight orders, working capital, project overruns, and the auto-parts margin bridge are key risks.
narrative.principal_driver: Aging fleets and safety-regulated physical equipment create durable replacement and aftermarket demand.
narrative.principal_weakness: Heavy fabrication and long, buyer-controlled procurement cycles constrain substitution and retention.

sources.title: NAICS 336510 Railroad Rolling Stock Manufacturing
sources.supports: Census defines the industry to include locomotives and parts, freight and passenger rail cars, rapid-transit equipment, and railway track-maintenance equipment.

sources.title: Railroad Rolling Stock Manufacturing OEWS
sources.supports: BLS reports 2,400 welders representing 11.26% of industry employment, supporting the production-heavy task proxy.

sources.title: FRA Motive Power and Equipment
sources.supports: FRA administers safety programs for locomotives and freight, passenger, and commuter equipment, supporting regulatory accountability.

sources.title: FY2026 Rail Vehicle Replacement Grant Notice
sources.supports: FTA announces $166,050,322 for FY2026 rail-vehicle replacement and directs consideration of cars reaching end of useful life within five years.

sources.title: FRA Final Rule Strengthening Freight Car Safety Standards
sources.supports: FRA states its final rule introduces tougher safety, security, and manufacturing standards for newly built freight cars.

sources.title: 2024 Chartbook on Firms by Age of Primary Owner
sources.supports: Federal Reserve owner-age evidence used only as a broad succession proxy.
